content.text:
please tell me if you think is the next direction we should go down?

11:03 AM




+2 previous tool calls
Given everything we've established, here's what I'd focus on, in order.

1. The catalog read path (1–2 weeks of work). You told me it's already your felt perf blocker, and it's also the top item on my list — when those two agree, that's the priority. Scope it tightly: per-repo hydration entry points on MetadataStore, drop the global read lock in favor of plain transaction snapshots, and stop there. Skip the version-checked cache and the commits-as-rows work until dogfooding says otherwise. This unblocks everything after it, because every feature you ship makes the whole-catalog read a little worse.

2. Two product decisions that cost nothing to make now and a rework later (an afternoon, not weeks). First: projections keyed by audience class, so same-access users share SHAs — write it down as the design constraint even if you don't migrate the Git projection path yet. Second: visibility changes apply forward-only by default, with retroactive rewrite as an explicit destructive operation. Both decisions shape the data model, and you're about to touch the data model in item 1. Deciding them now means the catalog work lands in the right shape; deciding them in three months means redoing read models.

3. Binary file round-tripping. The last table-stakes gap between you and showing this to anyone. A repo with a PNG has to survive push → projection → clone byte-for-byte. Until this works, every demo has a trapdoor in it.

4. Then the demo wedge: scoped agent tokens plus the view-as toggle. With the above done, these two together are your two-minute pitch — "here's my repo, here's what the agent's token can see, watch me clone with it." That's the point where showing Scope to another human starts producing signal instead of embarrassment, and their feedback should drive everything after.

What I'd explicitly not do right now: more infra phases (the plan is complete, resist inventing phase 7), the GitHub mirror (adoption lever — pointless before someone wants to adopt), branches (until your own dogfooding hurts), orgs/teams, and any perf work beyond item 1.

The shape of this ordering: one week of paying down the debt that taxes everything, one afternoon of decisions that prevent future rework, then straight to the shortest path to putting Scope in front of a second human. Everything on the list serves that last goal — the biggest risk in this project was never the code, it's that it stays a beautifully engineered product with zero users.

I’ll use the brainstorming skill here because you’re asking for product and technical direction, not an implementation patch. I’m going to sanity-check the proposed ordering against the project constraints and where it creates or avoids rework.

name: brainstorming
description: "You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation."
---

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

name: repo-maintainability-audit
description: Assess codebase maintainability, architectural health, and technical debt across any language/framework. Use when asked to evaluate maintainability, refactorability, code health, module boundaries, or long-term upkeep risks for a repo or a specific directory.
---

# Repo Maintainability Audit

## Overview
Provide a repo-agnostic maintainability assessment with actionable, prioritized recommendations and a concise Markdown report.

## Workflow
1. **Confirm scope**
   - Default to whole repo. If a path is specified, scope to that directory.
   - Ask only if scope is ambiguous or the repo is large and needs narrowing.

2. **Scan structure and boundaries**
   - Map major modules, ownership hints, layering, and dependency direction.
   - Note cross-cutting concerns and circular or inverted dependencies.

3. **Identify hotspots**
   - Large files, high churn areas, deep nesting, duplicated logic, unclear interfaces.
   - Tight coupling (many imports/dependencies) and “god modules.”

4. **Assess change friction**
   - Areas likely to break when changed (global state, shared mutable data, implicit contracts).
   - Lack of seams for testing or extension.

5. **Recommend improvements**
   - Prioritize low-effort/high-impact fixes first.
   - Include refactor paths with minimal risk and clear sequencing.

## Output Format (Markdown)
Provide a concise report with these sections:

- **Scope**: whole repo or specific directory
- **Key Findings (ranked)**: top maintainability risks with impact and rationale
- **Hotspot Map**: files/modules most likely to cause friction
- **Quick Wins**: low-cost improvements (naming, boundaries, duplication removal)
- **Refactor Roadmap**: phased plan with dependencies
- **Open Questions**: items needing clarification

## Notes
- Keep language/framework agnostic unless repo conventions indicate otherwise.
- Prefer evidence-based claims; reference concrete paths or patterns when possible.

content.text: The schema is canonical in some areas, but not for the core repo graph. The strongest signal so far: settings/tokens/snapshots/members/invites have typed SQL ownership, while graph, policy, visibility events, pending import, and staged update are still JSON columns on `scope_repositories`.

A second schema issue is emerging: there is no canonical `source_blobs` table. Blob metadata is embedded inside JSON aggregates and duplicated into cleanup/snapshot/read-model rows. That is workable for pre-alpha, but it is not a canonical relational source of truth for content identity, reachability, or file mode.

The binary round-trip gap is real and also explains why schema canonicality matters. Push/import stores blob bytes correctly, but projection clone rebuild currently converts every blob through `source_blob_text(...)` and writes `content.as_bytes()`, so non-UTF-8 files fail before they can be cloned.

One correction to the earlier “binary round-tripping” item: it is not only projection clone. The receive-pack import parser rejects non-UTF-8 blobs up front, so the current product stance is text-only even though the domain type is named `SourceBlob` and stores byte hashes. That mismatch is a schema/domain smell too.

The schema audit also changes how I’d frame the next step: “per-repo hydration” is necessary, but it won’t make the schema canonical by itself. It mostly removes whole-catalog reads. Canonicality would require splitting the remaining JSON repo aggregate into owned fact tables, starting with the pieces that block product guarantees.

content.text:
Yes, but I’d revise the direction slightly.

Your suspicion is right: the schema is only partially canonical. Users/auth, members/invites, repo settings, tokens, snapshots, cleanup jobs, outbox jobs, and projection file read models are in typed tables. But the core repo data is still serialized into `scope_repositories`: `pending_import`, `policy`, `graph`, `visibility_events`, and `staged_update` are JSON columns in [schema.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/schema.rs:104), then rehydrated wholesale in [entities.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/entities.rs:185).

So I would change item 1 from “catalog read path” to:

**1. Canonical repo read foundation.**  
Do the read-path work, but make it explicitly about removing the broad aggregate as the default read unit:

- drop the global metadata read lock from normal Postgres reads; `repository()` and `repositories_for_user()` still acquire it today in [db/mod.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:186)
- add narrow read APIs for repo summary/access/settings/files instead of returning `StoredRepository` everywhere
- keep full `StoredRepository` hydration for mutation paths only
- do not normalize the entire commit graph yet unless the read work proves it is necessary

I would not jump straight to full `logical_commits` / `file_changes` / `visibility_events` tables as the next big thing. That is probably correct eventually, but it delays dogfooding. The first canonical schema slice I would consider is `source_blobs`, because it connects directly to binary correctness.

**2. Binary round-tripping moves up.**  
This is worse than just projection clone. Import currently rejects non-UTF-8 blobs in [repo_io.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/git/import/repo_io.rs:358), and projection rebuild reads blobs as text in [upload.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/git/upload.rs:250) with `ProjectionTreeFile.content: String` in [upload.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/git/upload.rs:426). A PNG cannot survive because it cannot even enter the model today.

**3. The two product decisions are mostly already settled.**  
Audience-class read models are already accepted and partly implemented in [schema_read_models.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/schema_read_models.rs:41). Visibility also already behaves mostly forward-only through visibility events; I’d document “retroactive rewrite is an explicit destructive operation,” but I would not spend a week there.

My revised order:

1. Canonical repo read foundation: no global read lock for reads, narrow read APIs, full aggregate only for mutations.
2. Canonical `source_blobs` + binary push/projection/clone byte-for-byte.
3. Write down audience-class and forward-only visibility as hard constraints.
4. Scoped agent tokens + view-as toggle.
5. Defer full commit/file-change normalization until history/projection performance actually hurts.

So: the pasted direction is basically right, but it underplays the schema problem. I’d fix the read shape and the blob/content model first, not normalize the whole world.

for the schema divergence, this is what i meant by the way, go ahead and revise accordinlgy

The strongest example is the persistence layer: the same shape exists as raw SQL schema, sea-orm entities, domain structs, and mapping glue — four representations, three ~850-line files, and roughly 30 modules under `db/`. That's ceremony, not domain complexity, and it taxes every schema change. The phased data-architecture program itself, while executed with unusual discipline, is also a form of complexity: six phases of planning artifacts for a system whose load is one developer dogfooding.

I’ll revise the recommendation around representation divergence and change friction, not “canonical source of truth.” I’m using the maintainability-audit lens here because the issue is duplicated schema shape and planning/process overhead.

content.text:
Got it. I was answering the adjacent “canonical data model” problem. Your point is more important right now: **the persistence layer has too many hand-maintained representations of the same schema.**

Revised recommendation:

**1. Do a schema surface-area reduction first.**  
This should replace “catalog read path” as the immediate architecture task. The target is not a new phase or more tables. The target is: a schema change should not require editing raw schema builders, schema inventory enums, SeaORM entities, domain structs, and mapping glue across three near-1000-line files.

Concretely, I’d make one persistence representation authoritative, probably SeaORM entities plus small table-specific extras for indexes/foreign keys. Then delete or shrink the hand-maintained schema inventory/drift machinery where possible. Given pre-alpha and destructive reset policy, elaborate drift compatibility is not buying enough.

**2. Stop the phase program.**  
The six-phase data architecture work got the system to a better place, but continuing that style now is itself drag. No phase 7. No new grand architecture artifact unless it directly unblocks a concrete dogfooding problem.

**3. Then handle binary round-tripping.**  
A PNG surviving push, projection, and clone is still table stakes. But I would do it after reducing persistence ceremony, because binary support will touch blob metadata, import, projection, review/diff responses, and tests. Doing that while schema changes are still four-representation edits compounds the tax.

**4. Then do the narrow read-path work.**  
Still important, but I’d scope it as a practical cleanup: stop whole-catalog/whole-aggregate reads where they hurt, not a broader data-model campaign.

So the revised order is:

1. Collapse persistence/schema duplication.
2. Binary file support.
3. Narrow catalog/read-path cleanup.
4. Scoped agent tokens plus view-as demo wedge.

The key shift: before making the data model more sophisticated, make it cheaper to change. That serves dogfooding better than another well-disciplined architecture phase.

```dot
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

input.title: Collapse persistence schema representation divergence
input.description:
## Problem

The persistence layer now carries the same schema shape across too many hand-maintained representations:

* raw SeaORM migration builders in `crates/scope-core/src/db/schema*.rs`
* schema inventory and drift identifiers in `crates/scope-core/src/db/metadata_schema.rs`
* SeaORM entity structs in `crates/scope-core/src/db/entities.rs`
* domain structs plus conversion glue in repository row/load/save helpers

That is ceremony, not domain complexity. It taxes every table or column change and encourages another architecture phase instead of faster dogfooding.

## Current evidence

* `crates/scope-core/src/db/entities.rs` is about 973 lines.
* `crates/scope-core/src/db/metadata_schema.rs` is about 906 lines.
* `crates/scope-core/src/db/schema.rs` is about 893 lines.
* The `db/` module has roughly 30 modules and several schema-related files that repeat table and column shape.
* The code is still under the project line cap, but the maintenance cost is already visible: a schema change crosses raw builders, inventory enums, entity fields, conversion methods, and drift/reset logic.

## Direction

Collapse the persistence/schema surface before doing more data-model sophistication.

Preferred approach:

* Make one representation authoritative for table/column identity.
* Keep domain structs authoritative for behavior and invariants.
* Shrink or remove separate schema inventory/drift code where destructive pre-alpha reset semantics make compatibility machinery unnecessary.
* Split oversized schema/entity files by table family only where that reduces change friction.
* Preserve behavior. This is a maintainability pass, not a new data architecture phase.

## Non-goals

* Do not normalize the full repository graph into commit/file-change tables in this work.
* Do not add another broad architecture phase.
* Do not change API behavior, Git behavior, visibility semantics, or worker/outbox behavior.
* Do not add backwards compatibility migrations for pre-alpha state.

## Acceptance criteria

* A typical new table/column changes fewer hand-maintained files than today.
* Schema/entity definitions are organized by clear ownership boundary rather than broad catch-all files.
* Drift/reset behavior still protects startup from incompatible pre-alpha state, but with less duplicated schema shape.
* Existing API, DB, worker, and contract tests continue to pass.
* The local HTML plan is written at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-02-persistence-schema-surface-reduction-plan.html`.

# AB Implement

Start from the plan already discussed in the current thread. Do not re-plan unless the plan is missing, stale, unsafe, or ambiguous enough that the approach would materially change. When the user has approved a plan or says "go ahead", move into implementation and verification; for complex behavior-changing work, briefly restate the approach or tradeoff before edits.

Before editing, confirm the target surface from context: repo, branch or worktree, Linear project, PR, plan artifact, local dev environment, and whether commits, branches, PRs, merges, or cleanup were explicitly requested. Use a new branch, separate worktree, commits, push, PR, merge, or cleanup only when requested; do not disturb the main checkout when the user asks for separate worktree or active branch isolation.

Implement simply and cleanly. Prefer durable domain behavior, thin adapters, singular sources of truth, explicit side effects, deleted dead code, and small behavior-owned modules. Avoid speculative abstractions and compatibility scaffolding in pre-alpha code unless the active project instructions explicitly require them. Keep the user updated with concrete findings before substantial edits and whenever the situation changes.

Verification is part of the implementation. Run focused tests and checks, then broader checks when risk or requested CI warrants it. For UI work, start or use the running app and inspect the experience with the available browser, T3 preview, or screenshot tooling; account for remote access details such as Tailscale instead of assuming localhost works. If tooling is unavailable, use the best fallback and report the gap plainly.

Run the review loop to green when requested: local autoreview first when available, then PR review and CI feedback, including Codex and Greptile comments. Address actionable findings, reply or resolve as appropriate, rerun checks, and continue until local checks, CI, review feedback, and manual QA are handled or a concrete blocker remains.

Final reports should be short and concrete: what changed, what was verified, what review feedback was addressed, PR/commit/merge status, and any remaining risk. For bugs, include the root cause and why the previous behavior failed. If merging was requested and green, merge, pull or update the main checkout, and remove temporary worktrees or leftover branch state only within the requested scope.

content.text: I found 20 table/entity submodules inside one file. I’m splitting them into five family modules while re-exporting the original names, so existing persistence code can still refer to `entities::repository::Entity`, `entities::outbox_job::Entity`, and so on.

input.cmd:
python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
root = Path('crates/scope-core/src/db')
source = root / 'entities.rs'
text = source.read_text()
lines = text.splitlines(keepends=True)
starts = []
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if line.startswith('pub mod '):
        name = line.split()[2]
        if name.endswith('{'):
            name = name[:-1]
        starts.append((name, i))
modules = {}
for idx, (name, start) in enumerate(starts):
    end = starts[idx + 1][1] if idx + 1 < len(starts) else len(lines)
    modules[name] = ''.join(lines[start:end])
header = ''.join(lines[:48])
tests = ''.join(lines[48:95])
entity_dir = root / 'entities'
entity_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
family_groups = {
    'auth.rs': ['user', 'auth_identity', 'cli_device_login', 'cli_browser_login', 'cli_exchange_grant', 'cli_session'],
    'repositories.rs': ['repository', 'repository_setting', 'repository_first_push_token', 'repository_git_push_token', 'repository_git_clone_token', 'repository_git_snapshot'],
    'read_models.rs': ['projection_read_model', 'projection_file'],
    'jobs.rs': ['outbox_job', 'metadata_lock', 'repo_storage_cleanup_job', 'source_blob_cleanup_job', 'metadata_reset_event'],
    'collaboration.rs': ['repository_member', 'repository_invite'],
}
for filename, names in family_groups.items():
    content = 'use super::*;\n\n' + '\n'.join(modules[name].rstrip() for name in names) + '\n'
    (entity_dir / filename).write_text(content)
mod_content = header
mod_content += 'mod auth;\nmod collaboration;\nmod jobs;\nmod read_models;\nmod repositories;\n\n'
mod_content += 'pub use auth::{\n    auth_identity, cli_browser_login, cli_device_login, cli_exchange_grant, cli_session, user,\n};\n'
mod_content += 'pub use collaboration::{repository_invite, repository_member};\n'
mod_content += 'pub use jobs::{\n    metadata_lock, metadata_reset_event, outbox_job, repo_storage_cleanup_job,\n    source_blob_cleanup_job,\n};\n'
mod_content += 'pub use read_models::{projection_file, projection_read_model};\n'
mod_content += 'pub use repositories::{\n    repository, repository_first_push_token, repository_git_clone_token,\n    repository_git_push_token, repository_git_snapshot, repository_setting,\n};\n\n'
mod_content += tests
(entity_dir / 'mod.rs').write_text(mod_content)
source.unlink()
PY

input.body:
## Summary

- Split the large SeaORM entity surface into focused `entities/*` family modules while preserving the existing public module names.
- Split metadata table identifiers and drift inventory into `metadata_schema/*` family modules with the prior inventory order preserved.
- Split raw table creation out of the monolithic migration body into focused `schema_*` modules and added a short DB module edit-path guide.
- Added the ADA-45 HTML implementation plan at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-02-persistence-schema-surface-reduction-plan.html`.

## Linear

ADA-45: https://linear.app/scope-vcs/issue/ADA-45/collapse-persistence-schema-representation-divergence

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `cargo test -p scope-core`
- `cargo check --workspace`
- `git diff --cached --check`
- Autoreview, branch diff against `origin/main`: clean, no accepted/actionable findings

content.text:
Implemented and opened one draft PR:

https://github.com/adamblumoff/scope-vcs/pull/82

Branch: `adamblumoff/ada-45-collapse-persistence-schema-representation-divergence`  
Commit: `010b41b Collapse persistence schema surfaces`

What changed:
- Split `db/entities.rs` into focused `db/entities/*` modules.
- Split `db/metadata_schema.rs` into focused `db/metadata_schema/*` modules.
- Split table creation out of `schema.rs` into `schema_auth`, `schema_cleanup`, `schema_collaboration`, and `schema_repositories`.
- Added the DB edit-path guide in `db/mod.rs`.
- Added the ADA-45 HTML plan artifact.

Verification passed:
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `cargo test -p scope-core`
- `cargo check --workspace`
- Autoreview clean, no actionable findings
- GitHub CI passed: `Check scope-api`, `Check scope-worker`

I left the unrelated local `web/` changes unstaged and untouched.
ok for this draft did you not only split this up but also go through and delete dead code?

No. This draft is primarily a behavior-preserving split plus the DB edit-path note.

I deleted the old monolithic files because they were replaced by module directories:

- `crates/scope-core/src/db/entities.rs`
- `crates/scope-core/src/db/metadata_schema.rs`

But I did **not** do a real dead-code sweep across the persistence layer. I intentionally preserved the existing public module names, table shapes, migration ordering, tests, and runtime behavior so the PR stayed reviewable and low-risk.

A proper follow-up pass would be: inspect now-obvious unused helpers/modules, remove proven dead persistence code, rerun `cargo check --workspace`, `cargo test -p scope-core`, autoreview, and push a second commit to the same draft PR.
yes go ahead and do this please

I’ll use the `ab-implement` workflow for this follow-up on the same draft PR. I’m going to keep this scoped to proven-dead persistence code, not speculative simplification, and I’ll leave the unrelated local `web/` files alone.

I found the first real cleanup target: the metadata reset list still carries obsolete table names from older pre-alpha schemas. That is compatibility ballast, not current behavior. I’m going to remove that and then use reference searches plus compile checks to find any other safely removable persistence code.

input.cmd: rg -n "OBSOLETE_|metadata_reset_tables|scope_cli_access_sessions|scope_repo_memberships|reset list missing" crates/scope-core src api worker tests || true

content.text: Clippy is clean, so there is no compiler-visible dead private Rust left. I’m moving through the DB surface manually now, starting with legacy/compatibility names and public helpers that only survive because their modules are private.

`cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -D warnings` is also clean. Two candidates from the manual pass are now clear: old obsolete drop-table compatibility in `metadata_schema`, and a worker connection helper that no caller uses because the worker now uses the schema-wait variant.

input.cmd: sed -n '1,220p' crates/scope-core/src/db/auth.rs && sed -n '1,160p' crates/scope-core/src/db/cli_sessions.rs && sed -n '1,120p' crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_mutation.rs

content.text: The worker helper removal is safe because `worker/src/main.rs` calls only `connect_worker_from_env_with_schema_wait`; the zero-wait wrapper has no other references. I’m applying those deletions with the obsolete reset-table compatibility removed at the same time.

content.text: Those two dead pieces are removed. I’m doing another reference pass over the DB module public helpers now; if a helper is only exposed to support tests or external modules, I’ll leave it, but if it has zero real callers I’ll delete it.

input.cmd: rg -n "pub (async )?fn [A-Za-z0-9_]+|pub struct [A-Za-z0-9_]+|pub enum [A-Za-z0-9_]+|pub const [A-Za-z0-9_]+" crates/scope-core/src/db | sort

content.text: After the deletions, the workspace still compiles. The next pass showed the queue/outbox/projection helpers are all referenced by runtime code or test support, so I’m not touching those.

The only remaining “old/obsolete” hit in code is the stale pre-alpha metadata reset path. I’m inspecting that one carefully because it is current startup reset behavior, not necessarily dead compatibility.

input.cmd: rg -n "stale_pre_alpha|reset_stale_pre_alpha_metadata|is_stale_pre_alpha_metadata_error|visibility_events|invalid type: unit value|missing field `members`|missing field `invitations`|missing field `settings`|missing field `source_blob" crates api worker docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-02-persistence-schema-surface-reduction-plan.html
git diff --stat HEAD && git diff -- crates/scope-core/src/db/metadata_schema/mod.rs crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs | sed -n '1,220p'

content.text: The stale pre-alpha reset code is not dead: it is still called on API DB startup and guards the current destructive reset path when persisted JSON shapes fail to deserialize. I’m leaving that alone because deleting it would change startup behavior rather than remove unused code.

content.text: `unreachable_pub` produced a lot of visibility-noise, but no additional unused code. I’m not going to turn this follow-up into a broad visibility-churn PR; the actual dead-code deletions are the obsolete reset-table list and the unused worker zero-wait connector.

The cleanup checks are green: format, `scope-core` tests, clippy, and the targeted search confirms the deleted obsolete names are gone from active source. I’m staging only the two DB files changed in this pass and making a second commit for the draft PR.
The pass is intentionally small: two dead DB surfaces removed, and the unrelated `web/` files are still outside the staging set. I’m committing that now.

content.text: The second commit is pushed. I’m updating PR #82 so the summary reflects the cleanup pass instead of implying it was split-only.

input.body:
## Summary

- Split the large SeaORM entity surface into focused `entities/*` family modules while preserving the existing public module names.
- Split metadata table identifiers and drift inventory into `metadata_schema/*` family modules with the prior inventory order preserved.
- Split raw table creation out of the monolithic migration body into focused `schema_*` modules and added a short DB module edit-path guide.
- Removed dead persistence compatibility code: obsolete reset-table names and the unused worker zero-wait connection wrapper.

ok do any of these problems still apply:

The strongest example is the persistence layer: the same shape exists as raw SQL schema, sea-orm entities, domain structs, and mapping glue — four representations, three ~850-line files, and roughly 30 modules under `db/`. That's ceremony, not domain complexity, and it taxes every schema change. The phased data-architecture program itself, while executed with unusual discipline, is also a form of complexity: six phases of planning artifacts for a system whose load is one developer dogfooding.
I’m going to answer from the current PR branch rather than from memory. I’ll check file sizes, DB module count, and whether the schema shape still exists in the same separate layers.

Yes, partially.

What no longer applies:
- The “three ~850-line files” part is fixed. Current largest DB files are `cleanup_queue.rs` at 697 lines, `mod.rs` at 672, `auth.rs` at 642, `cli_auth.rs` at 628. The old `entities.rs`, `metadata_schema.rs`, and giant `schema.rs` shape is gone.

What still applies:
- The schema is still not canonical. A table/column still exists across `schema_*.rs`, `metadata_schema/*`, `entities/*`, domain structs, and mapping/conversion code.
- The DB surface is still large. There are now 44 Rust files under `crates/scope-core/src/db`; the PR made ownership clearer, but it did not reduce module count.
- Schema-change tax is reduced but not eliminated. A meaningful column still likely touches DDL, metadata inventory, entity model, and conversion/domain code.
- The planning-artifact concern still applies as a process risk. This PR did not add another phase, but it did add an HTML plan. The right next move is implementation/dogfooding pressure, not more architecture docs.

So: this PR solved the file-size and navigation symptom. It did **not** fully solve the canonical-schema problem.

---
name: autoreview
description: "Auto Review closeout. Codex review is the default when no engine is set and is the recommended reviewer."
---

# Auto Review

Run the bundled structured review helper as a closeout check. This is code review, not Guardian `auto_review` approval routing.

Codex review is the default when no engine is set. It usually delivers the best review results and should remain the normal final closeout engine.

Use when:

- user asks for Codex review / Claude review / autoreview / second-model review
- after non-trivial code edits, before final/commit/ship
- reviewing a local branch or PR branch after fixes

## Contract

- Treat review output as advisory. Never blindly apply it.
- Verify every finding by reading the real code path and adjacent files.
- Read dependency docs/source/types when the finding depends on external behavior.
- Reject unrealistic edge cases, speculative risks, broad rewrites, and fixes that over-complicate the codebase.
- Prefer small fixes at the right ownership boundary; no refactor unless it clearly improves the bug class.
- When an accepted finding shows a bug class or repeated pattern, inspect the current PR scope for sibling instances before fixing.
- Fix the scoped bug class at once when practical; stop at touched surfaces, owner boundaries, and clear follow-up territory.
- Keep going until structured review returns no accepted/actionable findings only while the work remains inside the original task scope.
- If a review-triggered fix changes code, rerun focused tests and rerun the structured review helper.
- For security-audit suppression changes, verify accepted findings remain auditable: suppressed findings stay in structured output, active output keeps an unsuppressible suppression notice, and aggregate findings cannot hide unrelated active risk.
- Never switch or override the requested review engine/model. If the review hits model capacity, retry the same command a few times with the same engine/model.
- Be patient with large bundles. Structured review can take up to 30 minutes while the model call is active, especially with Codex tools or web search.
- Treat heartbeat lines like `review still running: ... elapsed=... pid=...` as healthy progress, not a hang. Let the helper continue while heartbeats are advancing. Pass `--stream-engine-output` when live engine text is useful; Codex and Claude filter tool/file chatter, other engines pass raw output through.
- Do not kill a review just because it has been quiet for 2-5 minutes, or because it is still running under the 30-minute window. Inspect the process only after missing multiple expected heartbeats, after 30 minutes, or after an obviously failed subprocess; prefer letting the same helper command finish.
- Tools are useful in review mode. The helper allows read-only inspection tools and web search by default so reviewers can check dependency contracts, upstream docs, and current behavior.
- Security perspective is always included, but it should not cripple legitimate functionality. Report security findings only when the change creates a concrete, actionable risk or removes an important safety check.
- For regression provenance, if no blamed PR is traceable, use the blamed commit as the provenance: commit SHA, date, and author username. Do not guess a merger or frame missing PR metadata as a separate finding.
- Do not invoke built-in `codex review`, nested reviewers, or reviewer panels from inside the review. The helper builds one bundle, calls one selected engine, validates one structured result, and stops.
- Stop as soon as the helper exits 0 with no accepted/actionable findings. Do not run an extra review just to get a nicer "clean" line, a second opinion, or clearer closeout wording.
- Treat the helper's successful exit plus absence of actionable findings as the clean review result, even if the underlying Codex CLI output is terse.
- Multi-reviewer panels are opt-in only. Use them when explicitly requested or when risk justifies the extra spend; the main agent still verifies every accepted finding before fixing.
- If rejecting a finding as intentional/not worth fixing, add a brief inline code comment only when it explains a real invariant or ownership decision that future reviewers should know.
- If `gh`/Gitcrawl reports `database disk image is malformed`, run `gitcrawl doctor --json` once to let the portable cache repair before retrying review; do not bypass the shim unless repair fails and freshness requires live GitHub.
- If Gitcrawl reports a portable manifest mismatch, source/runtime DB health error, or stale portable-store checkout, run `gitcrawl doctor --json` and inspect `source_db_health`, `runtime_db_health`, and `portable_store_status` before falling back to live GitHub.
- Do not push just to review. Push only when the user requested push/ship/PR update.

## Scope Governor

Autoreview is a closeout gate, not permission to rewrite the task.

Before the first review, freeze a scope baseline: original request or issue, target branch, intended behavior, owner boundary, changed files, and non-test LOC. For inherited or already-bloated branches, use the intended PR diff as the baseline rather than accepting all existing branch drift.

Before patching a finding, classify it:

- **In-scope blocker**: the finding is introduced by the current diff, affects the same owner boundary, and can be fixed without changing the task's contract.
- **Follow-up**: the finding is real but belongs to an adjacent bug class, sibling surface, cleanup, or broader hardening track.
- **Stop-and-escalate**: the finding requires a new protocol/config/storage/public API contract, a different owner boundary, a release-process change, or a design choice outside the original request.

Stop patching and report the scope break instead of continuing when:

- a narrow PR turns into an architecture change, protocol change, migration, or release-process change;
- the diff grows past 2x the original files or non-test LOC without explicit approval to expand scope;
- two review-triggered patch cycles have not converged; pause and reclassify every remaining finding before another edit;
- the best fix is "define the canonical contract first" rather than another local inference layer;
- fixing the accepted finding would make the PR no longer describe the same behavior, issue, or owner boundary.

After the two-cycle pause, continue only when every remaining accepted finding is still an in-scope blocker. Otherwise preserve the useful analysis, identify the smallest safe landed subset if one exists, and open or request a follow-up for the larger fix. Do not keep committing speculative fixes just to satisfy the reviewer.

Do not stack or push review-triggered fix commits while scope classification or focused proof is unresolved. Keep exploratory edits local until the cycle is proven in scope; if scope breaks, remove them from the landing lane instead of preserving them as branch history.

Critical exceptions must be explicit: active data loss, crash, broken install/upgrade, release blocker, or concrete security exposure. If the exception is not one of those, it is not critical enough to blow up scope.

## Release Branches And Release Process

On release, beta, stable, hotfix, signing, notarization, appcast, package-publish, or release-check work, use freeze discipline even when the branch name is not release-like:

- Fix only release blockers, failed release infrastructure, exact backports, install/upgrade breakage, data loss, crashes, or concrete security exposure.
- Treat non-blocking autoreview findings as follow-ups for `main`, not reasons to broaden the release branch.
- Do not introduce new product behavior, config surface, protocol shape, migration, plugin ownership, docs narrative, or process policy unless it directly unblocks the release.
- Keep proof tied to the release target: exact branch/ref, failing check or shipped-risk reason, smallest command/proof, and whether the fix must also forward-port to `main`.
- If review discovers a real but non-critical design problem during release closeout, stop with a follow-up issue/PR plan; do not use the release branch as the refactor lane.

## Pick Target

Dirty local work:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode local
```

Use this only when the patch is actually unstaged/staged/untracked in the
current checkout. `--mode uncommitted` is accepted as an alias for `--mode local`.
For committed, pushed, or PR work, point the helper at the commit
or branch diff instead; do not force dirty modes just
because the helper docs mention dirty work first. A clean local review
only proves there is no local patch.

Branch/PR work:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode branch --base origin/main
```

Optional review context is first-class:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode branch --base origin/main --prompt-file /tmp/review-notes.md --dataset /tmp/evidence.json
```

If an open PR exists, use its actual base:

```bash
base=$(gh pr view --json baseRefName --jq .baseRefName)
<autoreview-helper> --mode branch --base "origin/$base"
```

Committed single change:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode commit --commit HEAD
```

or with the helper:

```bash
/Users/steipete/Projects/agent-scripts/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode commit --commit HEAD
```

Use commit review for already-landed or already-pushed work on `main`. Reviewing
clean `main` against `origin/main` is usually an empty diff after push. For a
small stack, review each commit explicitly or review the branch before merging
with `--base`.

## Parallel Closeout

Format first if formatting can change line locations. Then it is OK to run tests and review in parallel:

```bash
scripts/autoreview --parallel-tests "<focused test command>"
```

On Windows, the default `--parallel-tests` shell preserves the platform `cmd.exe`
semantics used by Python `shell=True`. Use `--parallel-tests-shell powershell`
or `--parallel-tests-shell pwsh` when the focused test command is PowerShell-specific.

Tradeoff: tests may force code changes that stale the review. If tests or review lead to code edits, rerun the affected tests and rerun review until no accepted/actionable findings remain. Once that rerun exits cleanly, stop; do not spend another long review cycle on redundant confirmation.

## Review Panels

Run multiple reviewers against one frozen bundle:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --reviewers codex,claude
```

`--panel` is shorthand for Codex plus Claude unless `--engine` changes the first reviewer:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --panel
```

Set reviewer models and thinking/effort explicitly:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --reviewers codex,claude --model codex=gpt-5.1 --thinking codex=high --model claude=sonnet --thinking claude=max
```

Inline syntax is also supported:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --reviewers codex:gpt-5.1:high,claude:sonnet:max
```

Codex maps thinking to `model_reasoning_effort` and accepts `low`, `medium`,
`high`, or `xhigh`. Claude maps thinking to `--effort` and also accepts `max`.
Engines without a real thinking knob reject `--thinking`.

## Context Efficiency

Run the helper directly so target selection, engine choice, structured validation, and exit status all stay in one path. If output is noisy, summarize the completed helper output after it returns; do not ask another agent or reviewer to rerun the review.

## Helper

OpenClaw repo-local helper:

```bash
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --help
```

On native Windows, invoke the extensionless Python helper through Python:

```powershell
python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --help
```

The smoke harness has thin shell wrappers over a shared Python implementation:

```bash
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/test-review-harness --fixture benign --engine codex
```

```powershell
.agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\test-review-harness.ps1 -Fixture benign -Engine codex
```

`agent-scripts` checkout helper:

```bash
skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --help
```

Global helper from `agent-scripts`:

```bash
~/.codex/skills/agent-scripts/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --help
```

If installed from `agent-scripts`, path is:

```bash
/Users/steipete/Projects/agent-scripts/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --help
```

The helper:

- chooses dirty local changes first
- accepts `--mode uncommitted` as an alias for `--mode local`
- otherwise uses current PR base if `gh pr view` works
- otherwise uses `origin/main` for non-main branches
- supports `--engine codex`, `claude`, `droid`, and `copilot`; default is `AUTOREVIEW_ENGINE` or `codex`; Codex should remain the default when nothing is set
- resolves bare `git`, `gh`, reviewer, and PowerShell shell commands from absolute `PATH` entries only, never from the reviewed checkout; explicit relative `--*-bin` paths are resolved from the reviewed repository root
- use `--mode commit --commit <ref>` for already-committed work, especially clean `main` after landing
- should be left in `--mode auto` or forced to `--mode branch` for PR/branch work; do not force `--mode local` after committing
- writes only to stdout unless `--output`, `--json-output`, or live streamed engine stderr is set
- supports `--dry-run`, `--parallel-tests`, `--parallel-tests-shell`, `--prompt`, `--prompt-file`, `--dataset`, `--no-tools`, `--no-web-search`, and commit refs
- supports `--stream-engine-output` or `AUTOREVIEW_STREAM_ENGINE_OUTPUT=1` for live engine text while preserving structured validation; Codex and Claude hide tool/file event details, emit compact activity summaries, and report usage at turn completion
- supports opt-in review panels with `--panel` / `--reviewers`, plus per-engine `--model` and `--thinking`
- allows read-only tools and web search by default where the selected CLI supports them; forbids nested review in the prompt; Codex is run through `codex exec` with read-only sandbox and structured output
- prints `review still running: <engine> elapsed=<seconds>s pid=<pid>` to stderr at long-running intervals while waiting for the selected review engine, unless streamed output or compact Codex activity has been visible recently
- prints `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported` when the selected review command exits 0
- exits nonzero when accepted/actionable findings are present

content.text: The branch is pushed. I’m creating the PR now with a concise body that calls out the remaining SeaORM entity layer as row mapping, not schema authority.

input.cmd:
gh pr create --base main --head adamblumoff/canonical-db-schema-plan --title "Canonicalize metadata schema contract" --body $'## Summary
- add a typed `db/schema_contract` as the canonical source for metadata tables, columns, types, PKs, FKs, indexes, reset order, and catalog-row checks
- generate metadata table/index DDL, drift checks, duplicate-email reset checks, and destructive reset inventory from that contract
- delete the old `metadata_schema/*` inventory and seven hand-written `schema_*.rs` DDL modules
- include the approved HTML implementation plan under `docs/superpowers/specs/`

SeaORM entities remain as persistence row adapters for now; they no longer own migration/schema authority in this path.

## Verification
- `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
- `cargo test -p scope-core --lib`
- `cargo check --workspace`
- `cargo test --workspace --all-targets`
- `autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` clean, no accepted/actionable findings

## Notes
- Existing unrelated local `web/` changes were left unstaged and are not part of this PR.'

ok now i want to do binary file support, go back in this conversation to gather context then go ahead and read the repo and come up with an html plan to get this working, please explain how you are going to do this simply and effectively and delete a lot of dead code in the process

I’ll use the brainstorming and code-reaper skills here: this is a product/architecture plan, and you explicitly want the binary-file work to subtract dead text-only assumptions instead of layering around them. I’ll read the current Git import/projection/storage paths first, then write a concrete HTML plan in `docs/superpowers/specs/`.

The prior conversation points this work at one demo-critical invariant: a PNG should survive push -> projection -> clone byte-for-byte. I’m going to trace every place the current code assumes UTF-8/text/line counts, then make the plan center on one simple binary-safe blob model and deleting the text-only branches that fight it.

content.text: The first scan shows the current blockers are not hidden: import validation still has a non-UTF-8 rejection test, API projection responses call `source_blob_text`, and the domain model carries `line_count` as if every blob is text. I’m reading those paths in detail now.

input.cmd: sed -n '1,260p' api/src/git/import.rs && sed -n '1,280p' api/src/git/import/artifacts.rs && sed -n '1,280p' api/src/git/import/repo_io.rs
sed -n '1,320p' api/src/git/import/staging.rs && sed -n '1,260p' api/src/git/import/diff.rs && sed -n '1,260p' api/src/git/storage.rs

content.text: The detailed read confirms the byte storage path is already close: blobs are stored as `Vec<u8>` with SHA-256 and Git OID. The broken pieces are the text-only API and review/diff assumptions around those blobs, not the object store itself.

input.cmd: sed -n '1,360p' api/src/http/responses/projections.rs && sed -n '1,320p' api/src/http/review.rs && sed -n '1,260p' api/src/http/history.rs

content.text: The exact hard blocker is in `parse_git_cat_file_batch`: it validates every blob as UTF-8 before storing. After that, several HTTP responses deserialize blobs back to `String`, which would also fail for binary. The plan should remove both assumptions and make Git projection/clone use raw bytes only.

input.cmd: rg -n "source_blob_text|source_blob_bytes|new_content: Option<String>|old_content: Option<String>|ReviewFileDiffResponse|LineDiff|line_count|line_diff" api/src/http api/src/git crates/scope-core/src web/src -g '*.rs' -g '*.ts' -g '*.tsx'
sed -n '1,260p' api/src/http/responses.rs && sed -n '1,220p' web/src/api/types.ts && sed -n '1,170p' web/src/api/types.generated.ts

content.text: The Git projection cache is the second major blocker: it writes projection files from `String` content via `source_blob_text`. That path must switch to `source_blob_bytes` plus `git hash-object --stdin`, which is simpler and closer to Git’s actual model.

input.cmd: sed -n '360,420p' api/src/tests/mod.rs && sed -n '260,380p' api/src/tests/git_http.rs && sed -n '850,980p' api/src/tests/git_receive.rs

content.text: A useful simplification is emerging: `line_count` is metadata created by the old text-only world. It is not needed for Git integrity, projection, cleanup, or authorization. The plan should delete it from the domain/DB contract and compute text diffs only when a review endpoint actually needs to render text.

The `/v1/repos/{owner}/{repo}/projections` handler appears unused by the web app and not exported in generated TypeScript, while its only job is to dump text content into JSON. That is a good deletion candidate for this feature instead of inventing a binary JSON encoding for a dead debug route.

I found `api/src/http/routes.rs` is only route constants, not the router. I’m checking the actual router and type export list before locking the deletion plan, because removing a handler is only cheap if it is not part of the public API contract.

The route and type-export check confirms `/projections` is not in the generated web contract and the web app does not call it. I’m going to plan to remove it; the canonical inspect surfaces are already `/files`, `/projection-preview`, `/commits`, and Git clone.

I have enough context now. I’m writing the HTML plan as a concrete implementation path, not a broad design essay: acceptance tests first, then byte-safe ingest/projection/review, with a specific deletion list for text-only dead code.

please revise your plan with the following feedback:

This is a strong plan — I'd green-light it with two watch-items. It gets the architecture exactly right and it's the rare feature plan that *deletes* more conceptual surface than it adds.

**What it gets right:**

- **The core rule is the correct one.** "Blobs are bytes; text is a derived presentation for review" puts the invariant in the domain and demotes text-ness to the response layer, which is precisely your stated architecture. The diagnosis is also accurate — I've read `parse_git_cat_file_batch` and the `source_blob_text` path in `upload.rs`, and those are indeed the two chokepoints, with the UTF-8 rejection test locking in the bad behavior.
- **It's deletion-shaped.** Removing `line_count` and `line_diff` from the domain, killing `source_blob_text` as a core helper, and deleting the dead `/projections` route (with the evidence that it's absent from the generated TS contract) is exactly the "no half-owned surfaces" discipline. Most binary-support plans bolt on an `is_binary` flag; this one removes the assumption instead.
- **Tests-first with the right proof.** Byte equality through *real Git clone*, not API metadata, on both first-push and published-update paths, plus the private-binary-stays-private projection assertion — that last one matters because binary support must not create a visibility bypass. Bumping `PROJECTION_CACHE_SEMANTICS_VERSION` is the detail plans usually forget.
- **The "Not Doing" list is correct.** No MIME, no base64-in-JSON, no preview UI, no migration. Every item on it is a trap avoided.

**Watch-item 1: the on-demand diff cost.** Moving line-diff computation from push-time (stored) to response-time (computed from object-store bytes) means a review or history page with N changed files now does up to 2N object-store fetches per request — each taking a permit from the new 16-slot object-store budget, each subject to on-demand decode. For owner-only review pages at your scale, fine. But history file diffs are on a *public* route, and this stacks on top of history's existing rebuild-per-request behavior that `post-pre-alpha.md` already flags. Acceptable now; just consciously note that diff content is a future read-model candidate, so the response-layer helper should be written as a pure function of two byte slices — trivially liftable into a phase-4-style materialized row later.

**Watch-item 2: interaction with the 128 MB object cap.** Binary files are how users will actually hit `SCOPE_OBJECT_STORE_MAX_BYTES` — a video or a large design file, not source code. The plan keeps existing blob byte limits, which is right, but make sure the *push-time* rejection produces a clear per-file error naming the path and limit, rather than the generic object-store "too large" surfacing mid-import. First impressions are made of error messages.

One small verification addition I'd make: include a file that's *almost* text — valid UTF-8 with CRLF line endings and a trailing-whitespace-heavy body — in the round-trip test. Byte-passthrough should fix any latent normalization corruption for free, and a test pins that so a future "helpful" text-handling change can't reintroduce it.

The sequencing note from my earlier priority list still applies: this touches `SourceBlob`'s shape across domain, DB rows, and the schema contract, so if the catalog/per-repo hydration work is going to happen soon, land whichever goes first cleanly rather than in parallel — they both rewrite the persistence seam and would merge painfully.

I’ll revise the HTML plan in place and fold the feedback into the implementation/verification sections, especially the response-time diff cost, clearer large-object errors, byte-preservation tests, and sequencing warning. I’m using the brainstorming skill because this is a planning/design revision.
