# UI Constraints

Treat all rules as binding. If a rule conflicts with a user request or project constraints, call out the conflict and ask how to proceed.

## General translation guidance
- If Tailwind is not in use, map Tailwind-specific rules to the closest equivalent in the project's design tokens, utilities, or CSS (keep the intent, not the class names).
- If no `cn` utility exists, add or use the project's equivalent classnames helper; prefer a `cn` wrapper around `clsx` (and `tailwind-merge` when Tailwind is used).
- If no z-index scale exists, define a minimal, named scale in theme/config (e.g., base, header, overlay, modal, toast) and reuse it.

## Stack
- MUST use Tailwind CSS defaults (spacing, radius, shadows) before custom values. If Tailwind is not used, use the project's default spacing/radius/shadow scales or design tokens before custom values.
- MUST use motion/react (formerly framer-motion) when JavaScript animation is required.
- SHOULD use tw-animate-css for entrance and micro-animations in Tailwind CSS.
- MUST use cn utility (clsx + tailwind-merge) for class logic.

## Components
- MUST use accessible component primitives for anything with keyboard or focus behavior (Base UI, React Aria, Radix).
- MUST use the project’s existing component primitives first.
- NEVER mix primitive systems within the same interaction surface.
- SHOULD prefer Base UI for new primitives if compatible with the stack.
- MUST add an aria-label to icon-only buttons.
- NEVER rebuild keyboard or focus behavior by hand unless explicitly requested.

## Interaction
- MUST use an AlertDialog for destructive or irreversible actions.
- SHOULD use structural skeletons for loading states.
- NEVER use h-screen; use h-dvh (use height: 100dvh when not using Tailwind).
- MUST respect safe-area-inset for fixed elements (use env(safe-area-inset-*) when not using Tailwind).
- MUST show errors next to where the action happens.
- NEVER block paste in input or textarea elements.

## Animation
- NEVER add animation unless explicitly requested.
- MUST animate only compositor props (transform, opacity).
- NEVER animate layout properties (width, height, top, left, margin, padding).
- SHOULD avoid animating paint properties (background, color) except for small, local UI (text, icons).
- SHOULD use ease-out on entrance.
- NEVER exceed 200ms for interaction feedback.
- MUST pause looping animations when off-screen.
- MUST respect prefers-reduced-motion.
- NEVER introduce custom easing curves unless explicitly requested.
- SHOULD avoid animating large images or full-screen surfaces.

## Typography
- MUST use text-balance for headings and text-pretty for body/paragraphs (use CSS text-wrap: balance/pretty if not using Tailwind).
- MUST use tabular-nums for data (use font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums if not using Tailwind).
- SHOULD use truncate or line-clamp for dense UI.
- NEVER modify letter-spacing (tracking-) unless explicitly requested.

## Layout
- MUST use a fixed z-index scale (no arbitrary z-x).
- SHOULD use size-x for square elements instead of w-x + h-x (use a single square utility/class if not using Tailwind).

## Performance
- NEVER animate large blur() or backdrop-filter surfaces.
- NEVER apply will-change outside an active animation.
- NEVER use useEffect for anything that can be expressed as render logic.

## Design
- NEVER use gradients unless explicitly requested.
- NEVER use purple or multicolor gradients.
- NEVER use glow effects as primary affordances.
- SHOULD use Tailwind CSS default shadow scale unless explicitly requested.
- MUST give empty states one clear next action.
- SHOULD limit accent color usage to one per view.
- SHOULD use existing theme or Tailwind CSS color tokens before introducing new ones.

I’d implement this as a real state, not a UI guess:

**Recommended design:** change `review.line_diff` from “always `{ additions, deletions }`” to either a computed diff or `null` when the API intentionally skips summary diffing. Then the review header renders:

- computed diff: `-12 +44`
- skipped diff: `Diff not summarized`

That keeps “actual zero” distinct from “too large / unavailable to summarize,” without adding more machinery. I’d also update the API tests that currently assert skipped summaries return `0/0`, plus the generated TS types and the review header component.

just make it "diff too large to summarize" for the skipped state, otherwise go ahead and make this commit real quick
I’ll make the skipped summary come through as `null` from the API and render exactly “Diff too large to summarize” in the header, then run focused tests and commit the change locally.
The contract is localized: two response structs, one helper, one review header component, and the tests that pinned the old `0/0` behavior. I’m going to patch those now.

One subtlety: mutation responses reuse the same staged-update response helper, so I’ll carry `Option<ReviewLineDiffResponse>` through those helpers too. That avoids falsely saying “zero” after visibility/apply/reject operations when the summary was skipped.

For the backend behavior I’m keeping read failures as `0/0` best-effort, because those are failure fallbacks. Only the intentional budget skip becomes `null`, which is what the UI will label as too large.

---
name: brainstorming
description: "You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation."
---

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

content.text:
Noted. I’d treat this as the right next architecture direction.

What changed since the earlier plan: the schema-canonicalization work did land. `schema_contract/*` is now the schema authority, and the old “four schema representations” problem is much reduced. The remaining problem is not schema ceremony so much as read shape: normal reads still ask for the full repo aggregate.

Current evidence:
- `MetadataStore::repository()` still takes the metadata read lock and hydrates `StoredRepository`: [mod.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:183)
- `repositories_for_user()` does the same for repo lists: [mod.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:218)
- `MetadataStore::read()` still loads the whole catalog for Postgres: [mod.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:131)
- API routes like repo list/detail/settings still consume those broad reads: [repos.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/repos.rs:28), [repos.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/repos.rs:91), [repos.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/repos.rs:274)
- The repository table still keeps `pending_import`, `policy`, `graph`, `visibility_events`, and `staged_update` as JSON: [repositories.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/schema_contract/repositories.rs:5)

My take: your proposed direction is correct, with one small caveat. “Full `StoredRepository` only for mutation paths” is right as a goal for product/UI reads, but Git projection/history/review still need graph-shaped domain data today. I would not force those paths into this first slice. Do repo list/detail/settings/files first, then let dogfooding tell us whether history/Git clone need materialized read models.

Recommended next slice:
1. Add narrow Postgres read APIs:
   `repo_summary`, `repo_summaries_for_user`, `repo_settings`, and `repo_live_files`.
2. Remove the metadata read lock from those APIs. Use a plain read transaction, ideally a consistent read snapshot for multi-query reads.
3. Refactor `list_repos`, `get_repo`, `get_settings`, and likely `/files` away from `find_repo()`.
4. Leave mutation, review, history, Git auth/projection, and publish/apply on full hydration for now.
5. Keep `source_blobs` as the next canonical-schema candidate, but only if it becomes the canonical blob metadata table, not a duplicate index beside JSON blobs.

I agree with not jumping straight to `logical_commits` / `file_changes` / full graph normalization. That would be a big detour. The smallest valuable move is to stop treating the repo aggregate as the default read unit.

Okay, so I'm just trying to understand this architecture. So right now when we call for a repo or whatever, whatever repo we're working on, we're locking up a global metadata thing, we're having a global metadata lock in the table. In one table. But this, oh what we should have is multiple tables so that we can have multiple locks for each table? Or is this a post-crisis limitation? Like I want you to explain like kind of the higher level of control decision that we're making here

Not exactly “multiple tables so we can have multiple locks.” The better framing is:

Scope still has a **catalog-style control model** in some paths. Postgres is capable of normal concurrent reads, but our code still says: “before reading repo metadata, touch this one global lock row so all metadata reads/writes coordinate through one place.”

That global lock is an application-level safety blanket, not a Postgres limitation.

Right now, for `repository()` / `repositories_for_user()` in Postgres, we do roughly:

1. Start transaction.
2. Acquire shared lock on one global `scope_metadata_locks` row.
3. Hydrate full repo aggregate: repo row, settings, tokens, members, invites, graph JSON, policy JSON, staged update JSON, etc.
4. Return `StoredRepository`.

Many reads can share that lock, but writes take the same row with a stronger lock. So unrelated repo work can still queue behind broad metadata operations. More importantly, every normal read pays the cost of loading “the whole repo brain” even if it only needs a name, access flags, or settings.

The control decision is:

**Move from global catalog coordination to scoped database facts plus narrow transaction snapshots.**

That means:
- Normal reads should not lock the global metadata row.
- Normal reads should read only the rows they need.
- Postgres transaction snapshots provide consistency for reads.
- Writes should rely on row locks, unique constraints, foreign keys, and domain mutation functions.
- If a write needs serialization, lock the specific repo row or specific affected rows, not the whole metadata universe.

Multiple tables help because they make facts addressable. For example:
- Repo summary reads repo row + member/access rows, not full graph.
- Settings reads repo row + settings row.
- File list reads projection file read-model rows.
- Mutation paths can still hydrate full `StoredRepository` because they need domain invariants.

So the architectural split is:

**Commands/mutations:** hydrate full domain aggregate, enforce rules, write canonical facts.

**Queries/reads:** use narrow read APIs shaped like the response, no full aggregate unless genuinely needed.

**Read models:** disposable, rebuildable tables for expensive views like projected file trees.

This is not “we need more locks.” It is “we need fewer artificial locks, smaller read units, and let Postgres do normal concurrency instead of pretending the DB is one giant in-memory catalog.”
ok do you think this is something we should do now, or wait until we have more features done?

I think we should do it now, but keep it narrow.

Reason: this is already touching the exact paths every future feature will lean on: repo list, repo detail, settings, files, review entry points. If we keep adding product features on top of “hydrate full repo aggregate under broad metadata coordination,” every feature makes the eventual read split harder and noisier.

I would not do the big version of it now:
- no full commit graph normalization
- no `logical_commits` / `file_changes` migration yet
- no broad read-model program
- no speculative cache layer

I would do the small foundation now:
- remove global read lock from normal Postgres repo reads
- add narrow read APIs for repo list/detail/settings/files
- leave full `StoredRepository` hydration for mutation, review, history, Git, publish/apply
- maybe add benchmark assertions around the hot routes so we can see the improvement

This is probably a 1-ish week architecture slice. It is worthwhile because it lowers the cost of everything after it without turning into a grand database rewrite. If we wait until more features land, the same work becomes a cross-cutting refactor through a larger surface area.

There’s a practical split in the first implementation: list/detail/settings can avoid full graph JSON immediately. `/files` can use the existing projection read model directly when it is fresh, but it still needs a deliberate fallback because stale/missing read-model rows are possible.

I’m checking the policy model before coding the summary reads; public repo detail needs to preserve the existing “public root or at least one visible projected file” behavior without decoding the graph on the happy path.

The policy check is simple enough to reuse: decode the small policy JSON for repo detail access, but avoid graph/pending/staged JSON decoding unless necessary. That preserves behavior while still skipping the expensive aggregate.

One important implementation detail: SeaORM entity loads would still pull the `graph` JSON column. I’m going to use `select_only` row structs for the narrow reads so we actually avoid the aggregate payload, not just avoid domain decoding.
I’m going to add a `repo_reads` DB module now. It will define read-shaped structs and Postgres/memory implementations for summaries, settings, and live files, then I’ll route the API through it.

autoreview findings: 2
[P2] Logged-in public viewers lose access to empty public repos
crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_reads.rs:633
`viewer_user_id.is_none()` treats every authenticated caller as non-public even when `access.actor` is `Public`. The old route path used `principal_for_scope_user`, which downgraded authenticated non-owners/non-members to `Principal::public()`, so a logged-in visitor could read a published repo whose root policy is public even when the public projection is empty. With this change, those same requests can return 404 for `GET /v1/repos/{owner}/{repo}` and `/files` unless `visible_projected_files` is true; `/settings` similarly returns 404 instead of the previous owner-required 403. Derive the public-viewer branch from `access.actor == RepositoryActor::Public` or otherwise preserve the old non-member-to-public principal behavior.

[P2] Default transactions no longer guarantee consistent aggregate reads
crates/scope-core/src/db/mod.rs:195
After removing the metadata read lock, `repository()` now relies on a default Postgres transaction while `repository_from_model` performs additional SELECTs for facts, members, tokens, and other rows. Postgres defaults to READ COMMITTED, where each statement can see a different committed version, so a concurrent repo mutation between the repository-row SELECT and the later fact/member SELECTs can hydrate a `StoredRepository` assembled from multiple versions. Existing callers still use this aggregate for access checks and update inputs, so this can produce incorrect authorization or settings decisions under concurrent writes. Use a transaction isolation level that pins one snapshot, or keep the read lock for this multi-query hydrate path.

overall: patch is incorrect (0.87)
The patch introduces a public-access regression for authenticated non-members and removes the read lock from multi-query aggregate hydration without replacing it with a transaction mode that provides a stable snapshot.

content.text: Autoreview returned two valid in-scope issues. One is an access parity bug for authenticated non-members; the other is that removing the global lock from multi-query aggregate hydration needs an explicit stable snapshot, not the default `READ COMMITTED` transaction. I’m fixing both now.

autoreview findings: 1
[P2] Postgres repo reads no longer canonicalize route owner/name
crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_reads.rs:360
The new Postgres lookup compares the raw path parameters against `owner_handle` and `name`. The previous `MetadataStore::repository(owner, name)` path used `repo_id(owner, name)`, which trims and lowercases both components, and the memory implementation still does that through `AppCatalog::repository`. As a result the converted read routes (`GET /v1/repos/{owner}/{repo}`, `/files`, `/settings`) now 404 on Postgres for requests like `/v1/repos/Owner/Repo` even though those paths previously resolved and sibling mutation routes still resolve through `repo_id`. Use the canonical repo id for this read lookup, or normalize the inputs before filtering.

overall: patch is incorrect (0.86)
The narrow read foundation is mostly consistent with the prior access checks, but the new Postgres owner/name lookup bypasses the repository id canonicalization used by the existing read path and by the memory implementation. That introduces a route behavior regression for non-canonical path casing on the converted repo read endpoints.

content.text: Second autoreview found one more valid parity issue: the new Postgres lookup used raw `owner_handle` and `name`, while the old aggregate path canonicalized through `repo_id`. I’m switching the narrow lookup to canonical repo id and adding coverage so mixed-case route params keep working.

The fix is small and clean: use `repo_id(owner, name)` for the lookup, then keep returning the stored owner/name casing from the row. This preserves canonical path behavior without adding another normalization layer.

input.cmd:
gh pr create --base main --head codex/canonical-repo-read-foundation --title "[codex] canonical repo read foundation" --body $'## Summary
- add narrow MetadataStore repo reads for list, detail, settings, and live files
- move normal repo routes off full StoredRepository hydration and delete the old broad MetadataStore::repositories_for_user API
- remove the global metadata read lock from single-repo aggregate reads and use repeatable-read/read-only snapshots for multi-query read transactions
- add SQL-shape, public non-member, and canonical repo-id regression coverage
- include the approved HTML implementation plan artifact

## Validation
- cargo fmt --all -- --check
- cargo test -p api repo_lifecycle
- cargo test -p api repo_visibility
- cargo test -p scope-core
- cargo test --workspace --all-targets
- pnpm -C web check
- ./dev/scope-dev doctor
- local autoreview: clean after fixes

## Notes
- ./dev/scope-dev bench is deferred to the manual QA phase because the dev stack was not running during pre-PR verification.'

content.text: The failure is a real CI lint gap: worker CI runs `cargo clippy -p scope-core -p worker --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings`, and it caught a helper that is only used in test builds. I’m removing that dead helper rather than suppressing the lint.

The helper is still needed for tests and the in-memory metadata backend, but it should not exist in a plain production build. I’m tightening the `cfg` boundary so production code only compiles the narrow Postgres read path.

---
name: autoreview
description: "Auto Review closeout. Codex review is the default when no engine is set and is the recommended reviewer."
---

# Auto Review

Run the bundled structured review helper as a closeout check. This is code review, not Guardian `auto_review` approval routing.

Codex review is the default when no engine is set. It usually delivers the best review results and should remain the normal final closeout engine.

Use when:

- user asks for Codex review / Claude review / autoreview / second-model review
- after non-trivial code edits, before final/commit/ship
- reviewing a local branch or PR branch after fixes

## Contract

- Treat review output as advisory. Never blindly apply it.
- Verify every finding by reading the real code path and adjacent files.
- Read dependency docs/source/types when the finding depends on external behavior.
- Reject unrealistic edge cases, speculative risks, broad rewrites, and fixes that over-complicate the codebase.
- Prefer small fixes at the right ownership boundary; no refactor unless it clearly improves the bug class.
- When an accepted finding shows a bug class or repeated pattern, inspect the current PR scope for sibling instances before fixing.
- Fix the scoped bug class at once when practical; stop at touched surfaces, owner boundaries, and clear follow-up territory.
- Keep going until structured review returns no accepted/actionable findings only while the work remains inside the original task scope.
- If a review-triggered fix changes code, rerun focused tests and rerun the structured review helper.
- For security-audit suppression changes, verify accepted findings remain auditable: suppressed findings stay in structured output, active output keeps an unsuppressible suppression notice, and aggregate findings cannot hide unrelated active risk.
- Never switch or override the requested review engine/model. If the review hits model capacity, retry the same command a few times with the same engine/model.
- Be patient with large bundles. Structured review can take up to 30 minutes while the model call is active, especially with Codex tools or web search.
- Treat heartbeat lines like `review still running: ... elapsed=... pid=...` as healthy progress, not a hang. Let the helper continue while heartbeats are advancing. Pass `--stream-engine-output` when live engine text is useful; Codex and Claude filter tool/file chatter, other engines pass raw output through.
- Do not kill a review just because it has been quiet for 2-5 minutes, or because it is still running under the 30-minute window. Inspect the process only after missing multiple expected heartbeats, after 30 minutes, or after an obviously failed subprocess; prefer letting the same helper command finish.
- Tools are useful in review mode. The helper allows read-only inspection tools and web search by default so reviewers can check dependency contracts, upstream docs, and current behavior.
- Security perspective is always included, but it should not cripple legitimate functionality. Report security findings only when the change creates a concrete, actionable risk or removes an important safety check.
- For regression provenance, if no blamed PR is traceable, use the blamed commit as the provenance: commit SHA, date, and author username. Do not guess a merger or frame missing PR metadata as a separate finding.
- Do not invoke built-in `codex review`, nested reviewers, or reviewer panels from inside the review. The helper builds one bundle, calls one selected engine, validates one structured result, and stops.
- Stop as soon as the helper exits 0 with no accepted/actionable findings. Do not run an extra review just to get a nicer "clean" line, a second opinion, or clearer closeout wording.
- Treat the helper's successful exit plus absence of actionable findings as the clean review result, even if the underlying Codex CLI output is terse.
- Multi-reviewer panels are opt-in only. Use them when explicitly requested or when risk justifies the extra spend; the main agent still verifies every accepted finding before fixing.
- If rejecting a finding as intentional/not worth fixing, add a brief inline code comment only when it explains a real invariant or ownership decision that future reviewers should know.
- If `gh`/Gitcrawl reports `database disk image is malformed`, run `gitcrawl doctor --json` once to let the portable cache repair before retrying review; do not bypass the shim unless repair fails and freshness requires live GitHub.
- If Gitcrawl reports a portable manifest mismatch, source/runtime DB health error, or stale portable-store checkout, run `gitcrawl doctor --json` and inspect `source_db_health`, `runtime_db_health`, and `portable_store_status` before falling back to live GitHub.
- Do not push just to review. Push only when the user requested push/ship/PR update.

## Scope Governor

Autoreview is a closeout gate, not permission to rewrite the task.

Before the first review, freeze a scope baseline: original request or issue, target branch, intended behavior, owner boundary, changed files, and non-test LOC. For inherited or already-bloated branches, use the intended PR diff as the baseline rather than accepting all existing branch drift.

Before patching a finding, classify it:

- **In-scope blocker**: the finding is introduced by the current diff, affects the same owner boundary, and can be fixed without changing the task's contract.
- **Follow-up**: the finding is real but belongs to an adjacent bug class, sibling surface, cleanup, or broader hardening track.
- **Stop-and-escalate**: the finding requires a new protocol/config/storage/public API contract, a different owner boundary, a release-process change, or a design choice outside the original request.

Stop patching and report the scope break instead of continuing when:

- a narrow PR turns into an architecture change, protocol change, migration, or release-process change;
- the diff grows past 2x the original files or non-test LOC without explicit approval to expand scope;
- two review-triggered patch cycles have not converged; pause and reclassify every remaining finding before another edit;
- the best fix is "define the canonical contract first" rather than another local inference layer;
- fixing the accepted finding would make the PR no longer describe the same behavior, issue, or owner boundary.

After the two-cycle pause, continue only when every remaining accepted finding is still an in-scope blocker. Otherwise preserve the useful analysis, identify the smallest safe landed subset if one exists, and open or request a follow-up for the larger fix. Do not keep committing speculative fixes just to satisfy the reviewer.

Do not stack or push review-triggered fix commits while scope classification or focused proof is unresolved. Keep exploratory edits local until the cycle is proven in scope; if scope breaks, remove them from the landing lane instead of preserving them as branch history.

Critical exceptions must be explicit: active data loss, crash, broken install/upgrade, release blocker, or concrete security exposure. If the exception is not one of those, it is not critical enough to blow up scope.

## Release Branches And Release Process

On release, beta, stable, hotfix, signing, notarization, appcast, package-publish, or release-check work, use freeze discipline even when the branch name is not release-like:

- Fix only release blockers, failed release infrastructure, exact backports, install/upgrade breakage, data loss, crashes, or concrete security exposure.
- Treat non-blocking autoreview findings as follow-ups for `main`, not reasons to broaden the release branch.
- Do not introduce new product behavior, config surface, protocol shape, migration, plugin ownership, docs narrative, or process policy unless it directly unblocks the release.
- Keep proof tied to the release target: exact branch/ref, failing check or shipped-risk reason, smallest command/proof, and whether the fix must also forward-port to `main`.
- If review discovers a real but non-critical design problem during release closeout, stop with a follow-up issue/PR plan; do not use the release branch as the refactor lane.

## Pick Target

Dirty local work:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode local
```

Use this only when the patch is actually unstaged/staged/untracked in the
current checkout. `--mode uncommitted` is accepted as an alias for `--mode local`.
For committed, pushed, or PR work, point the helper at the commit
or branch diff instead; do not force dirty modes just
because the helper docs mention dirty work first. A clean local review
only proves there is no local patch.

Branch/PR work:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode branch --base origin/main
```

Optional review context is first-class:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode branch --base origin/main --prompt-file /tmp/review-notes.md --dataset /tmp/evidence.json
```

If an open PR exists, use its actual base:

```bash
base=$(gh pr view --json baseRefName --jq .baseRefName)
<autoreview-helper> --mode branch --base "origin/$base"
```

Committed single change:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --mode commit --commit HEAD
```

or with the helper:

```bash
/Users/steipete/Projects/agent-scripts/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode commit --commit HEAD
```

Use commit review for already-landed or already-pushed work on `main`. Reviewing
clean `main` against `origin/main` is usually an empty diff after push. For a
small stack, review each commit explicitly or review the branch before merging
with `--base`.

## Parallel Closeout

Format first if formatting can change line locations. Then it is OK to run tests and review in parallel:

```bash
scripts/autoreview --parallel-tests "<focused test command>"
```

On Windows, the default `--parallel-tests` shell preserves the platform `cmd.exe`
semantics used by Python `shell=True`. Use `--parallel-tests-shell powershell`
or `--parallel-tests-shell pwsh` when the focused test command is PowerShell-specific.

Tradeoff: tests may force code changes that stale the review. If tests or review lead to code edits, rerun the affected tests and rerun review until no accepted/actionable findings remain. Once that rerun exits cleanly, stop; do not spend another long review cycle on redundant confirmation.

## Review Panels

Run multiple reviewers against one frozen bundle:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --reviewers codex,claude
```

`--panel` is shorthand for Codex plus Claude unless `--engine` changes the first reviewer:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --panel
```

Set reviewer models and thinking/effort explicitly:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --reviewers codex,claude --model codex=gpt-5.1 --thinking codex=high --model claude=sonnet --thinking claude=max
```

Inline syntax is also supported:

```bash
<autoreview-helper> --reviewers codex:gpt-5.1:high,claude:sonnet:max
```

Codex maps thinking to `model_reasoning_effort` and accepts `low`, `medium`,
`high`, or `xhigh`. Claude maps thinking to `--effort` and also accepts `max`.
Engines without a real thinking knob reject `--thinking`.

## Context Efficiency

Run the helper directly so target selection, engine choice, structured validation, and exit status all stay in one path. If output is noisy, summarize the completed helper output after it returns; do not ask another agent or reviewer to rerun the review.

```bash
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --help
```

On native Windows, invoke the extensionless Python helper through Python:

```powershell
python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --help
```

The smoke harness has thin shell wrappers over a shared Python implementation:

```bash
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/test-review-harness --fixture benign --engine codex
```

```powershell
.agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\test-review-harness.ps1 -Fixture benign -Engine codex
```

```bash
skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --help
```

```bash

```

ok very cool. off topic but can you explain to me sse's and how they work in the context of this repo,

I’ll ground this in the actual implementation rather than giving a generic SSE lecture. I’m going to read the repo event route, client usage, and state/event plumbing so the explanation matches Scope’s code.

I found the SSE route: `/v1/repos/{owner}/{repo}/events`. It is intentionally lightweight: it pushes “this repo changed” signals, and the web app refetches the normal read endpoints rather than receiving full repo data over the stream.

On the server side there are two layers: an in-process Tokio broadcast bus for live connections, and an optional Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY` bridge so multiple API processes can hear each other’s repo-change events.

The browser client deliberately does not use native `EventSource`; it uses `fetch` plus a streaming reader. That lets it attach the Clerk bearer token header, which plain `EventSource` cannot do reliably.

The tests confirm the key security behavior: public readers get redacted events with `version: 0`, member/owner readers get real change versions, and streams close if the repo becomes unreadable or deleted.

I have the full path now: route setup, server stream, change bus, Postgres fanout, client parser, and the tests. I’ll summarize it at the architecture level and point at the files where each piece lives.

**SSE Basics**

SSE means Server-Sent Events. It is a long-lived HTTP `GET` request where the server keeps the response open and writes small text messages over time.

A message looks roughly like:

```text
event: repo-change
data: {"repo_id":"owner/repo","version":2,"reason":"visibility-changed"}

```

The blank line ends one message. The browser/client reads each message as it arrives. Unlike WebSockets, SSE is one-way: server to client only. Client actions still happen through normal HTTP requests.

**How Scope Uses It**

In this repo, SSE is used as a live refresh signal for repository pages. It does not stream full repo data. It just says: “this repo changed, refetch your normal data.”

Flow:

1. The web route for `/repos/$owner/$repo` loads live state and calls `useRepoLiveRefresh(...)` in [web/src/routes/repos.$owner.$repo.tsx](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/web/src/routes/repos.$owner.$repo.tsx:21).

2. The hook opens the repo event stream in [repo-live-refresh.ts](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/web/src/features/repo-detail/repo-live-refresh.ts:16).

3. The API route is `/v1/repos/{owner}/{repo}/events`, implemented in [api/src/http/repo_events.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/repo_events.rs:19).

4. When a mutation happens, code calls `state.publish_repo_change(...)`, for example visibility changes, settings changes, publish, staged update apply/reject, Git push, or delete. The central helper is in [api/src/state.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/state.rs:180).

5. That publishes into `RepoChangeBus`, which is a per-repo Tokio broadcast channel in [crates/scope-core/src/repo_events.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/repo_events.rs:44).

6. The web client receives the event and calls `router.invalidate()`, which makes TanStack Router refetch the current route data.

**Important Design Detail**

Scope uses `fetch()` with a streaming reader instead of native `EventSource`, because the client needs to attach an `Authorization: Bearer ...` header from Clerk. Native `EventSource` is awkward for auth headers.

**Security Behavior**

The stream re-checks access on each event. If the repo becomes unreadable, the stream closes.

Owners/members get real versions and reasons:

```json
{"repo_id":"owner/repo","version":2,"reason":"visibility-changed"}
```

Public readers get redacted events:

```json
{"repo_id":"owner/repo","version":0,"reason":"repo-changed"}
```

That avoids leaking private change versions or exact private activity to public viewers.

**Reliability Behavior**

This is not a durable event log. It is a live notification channel.

If the client misses messages or the broadcast buffer overflows, the server sends a synthetic `"lagged"` event. The client treats that as “I may be stale” and force-refetches.

There is also a 20-second keepalive in [api/src/http/repo_events.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/http/repo_events.rs:84), so proxies/browsers do not assume the connection is dead.

For multiple API processes, Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY` fans repo-change notifications across instances in [repo_change_notifications.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/db/repo_change_notifications.rs:12). Locally/in-memory, it is just the in-process bus.

ok thanks for the explanation, can you go ahead and investigate this problem and let me know what you think?

**The SHA-divergence problem is your existential product question.** Projected commit IDs are keyed per-principal (`pv_{principal_id}_{commit}_{seq}` in `projection.rs`), so two members with *identical* access still get different Git histories and different SHAs. That breaks the most basic collaboration primitive: "look at commit abc123." Consider keying projections by visibility-equivalence class instead of principal — everyone with the same effective view shares one projection. That's simultaneously a product win (shareable SHAs, working PR discussions), a performance win (shared projection cache), and a cost win (one bare repo per class instead of per principal). I'd rank this above everything else.

I’ll investigate it against the actual projection and Git cache code. The core question is whether projected commit IDs are really principal-keyed today, whether that maps to real Git SHAs, and what would have to change to make “same effective view, same history” true.

---
name: brainstorming
description: "You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation."
---

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

content.text: Using the brainstorming skill lightly here because this is an architecture/product direction question, not an implementation request. I’m going to stop at investigation and recommendation, not write a spec or change code.

The problem is real at the domain projection layer: `projected_id()` includes the principal-derived string, and that ID is used as an input to the Git projection cache. I’m checking whether the actual Git commit object SHA also diverges, because that depends on how `ProjectedCommit.projected_id` is written into the synthetic Git repo.

There’s one nuance: the synthetic Git commit SHA is not directly using `projected_id` in the commit message. But the cache key does, and preview/history IDs do. Also, if two users have different visible content because access differs, Git SHAs must differ. The bug is specifically that equivalent access classes are not represented as first-class keys.

Important correction emerging: the quoted claim is partly right, but it overstates the Git impact. The domain `ProjectedCommit.projected_id` diverges by principal, but the Git commit writer does not put that ID into the actual Git commit object. For identical visible trees/messages, the Git SHAs should still converge; the duplicated cache is the obvious current waste.

There is a bigger divergence than the quote names: owners with Git credentials get the raw Git snapshot when available, while members get a synthetic projection. So owner/member private clones can diverge even when both can see every file. That is more product-critical than the `pv_{principal}` naming alone.

The original Git snapshot is stored as a bundle, while the domain graph stores simplified logical commits. That means “canonical Git identity” currently lives in two places: raw Git bundle for owner clone, and synthetic projected histories for non-owner reads. That split is the real architectural smell.

content.text: The existing read-model work already moved one slice in the right direction: projection file rows are keyed by `audience = private|public`, not by user. Git projection cache and domain projected IDs have not been brought into that same audience-class model yet.

There are tests pinning that owner upload-pack serves the raw bucket-backed Git snapshot head. So preserving owner raw SHA is intentional today. That means changing SHA semantics is not just a cache-key cleanup; it is a product decision about whether private collaborators should see raw Git history or Scope-synthesized history.

I’ve got enough to answer. The short version is: yes, this deserves a high ranking, but I’d revise the diagnosis. The actual existential issue is “what is the canonical Git identity for an audience?” not merely “principal is in `projected_id`.”

Yes, I think this is a real product-level issue, but I’d revise the diagnosis.

The quote is right that `projected_id` is principal-keyed: [projection.rs](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/crates/scope-core/src/domain/projection.rs:229) creates IDs like `pv_{principal}_{source}_{seq}`. That affects API history IDs, preview IDs, and the projection cache key.

But actual Git SHAs are subtler. The synthetic Git commit writer does **not** put `projected_id` into the Git commit object. It writes deterministic commits from tree + parent + message. So two members with identical synthetic projections may still get identical Git SHAs, but they pay for separate projection cache entries because [projection_cache_key](/home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs/api/src/git/upload.rs:313) hashes `principal_id` and `projected_id`.

The bigger issue is this: owners get the raw Git snapshot when possible, while members get a synthetic projection. That means owner and member can have identical effective access to all files but still see different Git histories/SHAs. That is the collaboration-breaking version of the problem.

I’d frame the real decision as:

**Canonical Git identity should be keyed by audience/effective-view, not by user.**

Current state:
- File read models already do this with `private` / `public` audiences.
- API history mostly talks in `owner` / `public`.
- Git projection cache still uses principal-shaped projection objects.
- Owner Git clone uses raw snapshot; member clone uses synthetic projection.

My recommendation: this should be the next architectural product decision before scoped agent tokens. Not necessarily a huge implementation immediately, but decide the rule now.

The rule I’d choose:
- `public` audience: synthetic public projection.
- `private` audience: canonical full-private view shared by owner + members who can read private files.
- Future scoped tokens: each distinct visibility scope gets a stable class key, not a token/user key.

Then decide whether `private` should use raw Git snapshot or synthetic projection. I lean raw snapshot for private/full-access, because it preserves normal Git collaboration: owner and private collaborators share real commit SHAs. Synthetic projections should be for restricted views.

So yes: rank it high. I’d put it above more feature work, but I’d plan it carefully because it touches Git identity, history URLs, projection cache invalidation, and clone semantics.

I’ll turn the recommendation into an executable HTML plan, grounded in the current files. I’m going to reuse the existing `docs/superpowers/specs` style so it lands beside the other architecture plans.

I’m going to make the plan opinionated around one product rule: default clone/history is a live audience view, and `private` is shared by owner plus full-read members. That also means deleting the current owner-only raw/staged clone behavior rather than preserving two meanings for “private.”

content.text: The remaining references are exactly where expected: API history/preview routing, Git upload/storage, generated web types, and tests. I’m going to update the shared vocabulary first, then run checks to catch the long tail.

One concrete mismatch is already visible: the response builder uses `view_key`, but the response struct still declares `principal_id`. I’m fixing the API structs and route logic now so the contract is internally consistent before touching the web client.

I’m making the behavioral change in the Git path at the same time as the naming change: private readers will share the same live raw snapshot when one exists, and staged snapshots will no longer leak into default clone reads.

content.text: The clone shortcut now keys off the effective private view rather than ownership. That means a collaborator with full private-file read access takes the same raw-snapshot path as the owner, while public readers still get a redacted projection.

content.text: I’m also updating receive-pack seeding so the fallback projection uses the same view-class rule. That keeps push staging from reintroducing principal-specific projection IDs when there is no raw Git snapshot to seed from.

content.text: The Rust domain and Git paths are now on the new identity shape. Next I’m moving the HTTP/web vocabulary from `owner` audience to `private` audience, keeping repo owner handles untouched.

The UI has two separate concepts named “owner”: repo owner parameters and projection audience. I’m only changing the audience concept, so route params like `$owner` and owner handles stay as they are.

fn declaration<T: TS>(config: &ts_rs::Config) -> String {
    format!("export {}", T::decl(config))
}

fn generated_header() -> String {
    [
        "// This file is generated from Rust API response/request types.",
        "// Run `cargo test --manifest-path api/Cargo.toml export_api_types -- --ignored` to update it.",
        "// Do not edit this file by hand.",
    ]
    .join("\n")
}

fn cli_auth_endpoint_declarations() -> String {
    let endpoints = [
        ("accountSession", routes::ACCOUNT_SESSION),
        ("cliSession", routes::CLI_SESSION),
        ("deviceLoginStart", routes::CLI_DEVICE_LOGIN),
        ("deviceLoginPoll", routes::CLI_DEVICE_LOGIN_POLL),
        ("browserLoginStart", routes::CLI_BROWSER_LOGIN),
        ("browserLoginExchange", routes::CLI_BROWSER_LOGIN_EXCHANGE),
        (
            "exchangeGrantExchange",
            routes::CLI_EXCHANGE_GRANTS_EXCHANGE,
        ),
    ];
    let body = endpoints
        .into_iter()
        .map(|(name, path)| format!("  {name}: \"{path}\","))
        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
        .join("\n");
    format!("export const CliAuthApiEndpoints = {{\n{body}\n}} as const;")
import { spawnSync } from 'node:child_process'
import { mkdtemp, readFile, rm } from 'node:fs/promises'
import os from 'node:os'
import path from 'node:path'
import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'

const webRoot = path.resolve(path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)), '..')
const repoRoot = path.resolve(webRoot, '..')
const apiManifest = path.join(repoRoot, 'api', 'Cargo.toml')
const generatedTypesPath = path.join(webRoot, 'src', 'api', 'types.generated.ts')
const tempDir = await mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'scope-api-types-'))
const tempTypesPath = path.join(tempDir, 'types.generated.ts')

try {
  const result = spawnSync(
    'cargo',
    ['test', '--manifest-path', apiManifest, 'export_api_types', '--', '--ignored'],
    {
      cwd: repoRoot,
      env: {
        ...process.env,
        SCOPE_API_TS_EXPORT_PATH: tempTypesPath,
      },
      stdio: 'inherit',
    },
  )

  if (result.status !== 0) {
    process.exit(result.status ?? 1)
  }

  const [checkedIn, generated] = await Promise.all([
    readFile(generatedTypesPath, 'utf8'),
    readFile(tempTypesPath, 'utf8'),
  ])

  if (normalizeLineEndings(checkedIn) !== normalizeLineEndings(generated)) {
    console.error('Generated API types are stale.')
    console.error(
      'Run `cargo test --manifest-path api/Cargo.toml export_api_types -- --ignored` from the repo root.',
    )
    process.exit(1)
  }
} finally {
  await rm(tempDir, { force: true, recursive: true })
}

function normalizeLineEndings(source) {
  return source.replace(/\r\n?/g, '\n')
}

content.text: Next I’m updating tests in the same direction: old tests that passed a `Principal` into projection now pass an explicit `ProjectionViewKey`, and API tests that asked for `audience=owner` now ask for `audience=private`.

I’m going to use a narrow mechanical rewrite for test call sites: `Principal::public() + false` becomes `ProjectionViewKey::Public`, and full-reader private projections become `ProjectionViewKey::Private`. Then I’ll clean imports and let `cargo check` catch anything non-obvious.

content.text: I’m replacing the direct-path raw snapshot assertion with a real HTTP clone assertion. The test will create owner and member clone tokens, publish a live snapshot, stage a different snapshot, then clone both and verify both heads equal the live head, not the staged head.
I’m deleting the old staged-snapshot expectation instead of weakening it. The new rule is default clone reads the live view only; staged content remains review-only.

content.text: The new real clone test failed, which is useful: the clone did not get the raw live snapshot head. I’m checking whether it fell back to the synthetic projection or accidentally still saw staged state, then I’ll tighten the route behavior.

The failed clone took the public projection before Git sent credentials. That can happen because the repo is public: Git accepts the anonymous advertisement and never has to retry with Basic auth. I’m adjusting the regression to force the credentialed private clone path, which is the behavior this change is meant to protect.

name: "yeet"
description: "Publish local changes to GitHub by confirming scope, committing intentionally, pushing the branch, and opening a draft PR through the GitHub app from this plugin, with `gh` used only as a fallback where connector coverage is insufficient."
---

# GitHub Publish Changes

## Overview

Use this skill only when the user explicitly wants the full publish flow from the local checkout: branch setup if needed, staging, commit, push, and opening a pull request.

This workflow is hybrid:

- Use local `git` for branch creation, staging, commit, and push.
- Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for pull request creation after the branch is on the remote.
- Use `gh` as a fallback for current-branch PR discovery, auth checks, or PR creation when the connector path cannot infer the repository or head branch cleanly.

## Prerequisites

- Require GitHub CLI `gh`. Check `gh --version`. If missing, ask the user to install `gh` and stop.
- Require authenticated `gh` session. Run `gh auth status`. If not authenticated, ask the user to run `gh auth login` (and re-run `gh auth status`) before continuing.
- Require a local git repository with a clean understanding of which changes belong in the PR.

## Naming conventions

- Branch: `codex/{description}` when starting from main/master/default.
- Commit: `{description}` (terse).
- PR title: `[codex] {description}` summarizing the full diff.

## Workflow

1. Confirm intended scope.
   - Run `git status -sb` and inspect the diff before staging.
   - If the working tree contains unrelated changes, do not default to `git add -A`. Ask the user which files belong in the PR.
2. Determine the branch strategy.
   - If on `main`, `master`, or another default branch, create `codex/{description}`.
   - Otherwise stay on the current branch.
3. Stage only the intended changes.
   - Prefer explicit file paths when the worktree is mixed.
   - Use `git add -A` only when the user has confirmed the whole worktree belongs in scope.
4. Commit tersely with the confirmed description.
5. Run the most relevant checks available if they have not already been run.
   - If checks fail due to missing dependencies or tools, install what is needed and rerun once.
6. Push with tracking: `git push -u origin $(git branch --show-current)`.
7. Open a draft PR.
   - Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for PR creation after the push succeeds.
   - Derive `repository_full_name` from the remote, for example by normalizing `git remote get-url origin` or by using `gh repo view --json nameWithOwner`.
   - Derive `head_branch` from `git branch --show-current`.
   - Derive `base_branch` from the user request when specified; otherwise use the remote default branch, for example via `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef`.
   - If the branch is being pushed from a fork or the PR target differs from the remote that was just pushed, prefer `gh pr create` fallback because the connector PR creation flow expects one repository target and may not encode cross-repo head semantics cleanly.
   - If connector-based PR creation cannot infer the repository or branch cleanly, fall back to `gh pr create --draft --fill --head $(git branch --show-current)`.
   - Write the PR body to a temp file with real newlines when using CLI fallback so the markdown renders cleanly.
8. Summarize the result with branch name, commit, PR target, validation, and anything the user still needs to confirm.

## Write Safety

- Never stage unrelated user changes silently.
- Never push without confirming scope when the worktree is mixed.
- Default to a draft PR unless the user explicitly asks for a ready-for-review PR.
- If the repository does not appear to be connected to an accessible GitHub remote, stop and explain the blocker before making assumptions.

## PR Body Expectations

The PR description should use real Markdown prose and cover:

- what changed
- why it changed
- the user or developer impact
- the root cause when the PR is a fix
- the checks used to validate it

input.title: [codex] Share projection identity by view
input.body:
## Summary

- Replace per-principal projected Git identity with shared `private` and `public` projection view keys.
- Rename projection/history API and web audience vocabulary from `owner` to `private`, with `view_key` replacing projection response `principal_id`.
- Let private readers share the live raw Git snapshot path and stop serving staged snapshots from default clone reads.
- Add regression coverage for owner/member shared private projected IDs, shared projection cache keys, and real Git clone head equality.
- Include the HTML implementation plan for the shared projection identity work.

## Validation

- `cargo test --manifest-path api/Cargo.toml -- --nocapture`
- `pnpm --dir web check`
- `cargo test --workspace -- --nocapture`
- `pnpm --dir web typecheck`
- `git diff --check`

- Shared projection identity is now keyed by `private` / `public` view, not principal.
- API/web vocabulary moved from `owner` audience to `private`.
